tweets.title:
General N. S. Raja Subramani Takes Charge as Chief of Defence Staff

With a career starting in 1985 in the Garhwal Rifles, the new CDS brings extensive experience from operational roles in Jammu & Kashmir and Assam, alongside senior positions as Vice Chief of the Army Staff and Military Adviser to the NSCS. He is highly decorated, having received the PVSM, AVSM, SM, and VSM for his service.

General Subramani’s mandate focuses on critical reforms within the Indian Armed Forces:
- Theaterisation: Implementing integrated military commands.
- Jointness: Enhancing synergies across the Army, Navy, and Air Force.
-Indigenization: Accelerating Atmanirbharta (self-reliance) in defense production.

RT by @OsintTV: सप्त शक्ति कमान के सेना कमांडर लेफ्टिनेंट जनरल मंजिंदर सिंह ने की DOT डिवीजन की युद्धक तैयारियों की समीक्षा, स्वदेशी सैन्य नवाचारों का किया अवलोकन, अत्याधुनिक काउंटर-ड्रोन ट्रेनिंग नोड का उद्घाटन कर भविष्य की चुनौतियों के लिए सेना को किया और मजबूत

In a significant boost to the Indian Army's modernization and operational preparedness efforts, Army Commander of Sapta Shakti Command, Lt Gen Manjinder Singh, conducted a comprehensive review of the operational readiness of DOT Division. The visit highlighted the Army's commitment to enhancing combat effectiveness through innovation, indigenous technology, and advanced military training.

During the interaction, troops showcased a range of indigenous innovations developed to improve battlefield performance, force protection, surveillance capabilities, and mission effectiveness. These soldier-driven technological solutions reflect the Indian Army's growing focus on self-reliance and innovation under the vision of Atmanirbhar Bharat.

A major highlight of the visit was the inauguration of a state-of-the-art Counter Unmanned Aerial Systems (C-UAS) Training Node. The facility will play a crucial role in preparing soldiers to detect, track, and neutralize emerging drone threats, a capability that has become increasingly important in modern warfare and border security operations.

Lt Gen Manjinder Singh commended the professionalism, dedication, and innovative spirit displayed by all ranks. Recognizing exceptional contributions, he also presented awards to deserving personnel for their outstanding performance and commitment to operational excellence.

The visit underscores the Indian Army's continued transformation into a future-ready force capable of addressing evolving security challenges through technology, innovation, and superior combat preparedness. As drone warfare, AI-enabled systems, and next-generation battlefield technologies reshape modern conflicts, initiatives like these ensure that the Indian Army remains prepared, adaptive, and mission-ready.

OSINT ASSESSMENT:

Operation Sindoor left Pakistan with a serious deep scar. They claimed strikes deep inside India but failed to produce a single credible satellite image to support those claims.

Now comes PRSC-EO3.

Looks like the real mission is not surveillance, but content creation for the next press conference. If Op Sindoor 2.0 ever happens, expect plenty of fake satellite imagery for domestic consumption, regardless of what actually happened on the ground.

Some countries build capabilities for warfare. Others build capabilities to explain away its outcome.

Interestingly, PRSC-EO3 was launched on April 25, 2026, with the help of China's Taiyuan Satellite Launch Center, barely a year after Operation Sindoor exposed the credibility gap in Pakistan's battlefield claims.

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: An Enduring Legacy

Though India is gradually replacing liquid-fueled Prithvi variants with faster-reacting, solid-fueled systems such as Prahaar and Pralay, the missile remains a landmark achievement. Produced in series by Bharat Dynamics Limited and deployed across multiple regiments, Prithvi proved that India could master complex missile technology and laid the foundation for the nation’s rise as a major missile power.

14/14

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: Beyond Strike Missions

Prithvi’s legacy extends far beyond offensive operations. Its airframe served as the basis for the Prithvi Air Defence (PAD) interceptor, a critical component of India’s Ballistic Missile Defence programme. Knowledge gained from Prithvi also contributed directly to the development of the Agni missile family.

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: From Pokhran-II to Strategic Deterrence

Following India’s 1998 nuclear tests at Pokhran, Prithvi emerged as one of the country’s key nuclear delivery systems. With the establishment of the Strategic Forces Command in 2003, operational control of nuclear-capable Prithvi units became integrated into India’s formal nuclear command structure.

R to @NetramDefence: Dhanush: Taking Prithvi to Sea

The Dhanush missile adapted the Prithvi design for naval operations. Launched from a hydraulically stabilized platform aboard warships, it provided the Indian Navy with a sea-based ballistic missile capability and represented an important step toward a more survivable strategic deterrent.

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: Prithvi-II: Strategic Reach

Developed primarily for the Indian Air Force and later operated by the Strategic Forces Command, Prithvi-II traded payload weight for increased range. With a reach of 250–350 km and a payload capacity of 500–1,000 kg, it incorporated features designed to complicate enemy missile-defense efforts.

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: Prithvi-I: The Army’s Workhorse

Inducted into the Indian Army in 1994, Prithvi-I (SS-150) featured a range of 150 km and could carry a payload of up to 1,000 kg. Designed for battlefield use, it was capable of delivering both conventional and tactical nuclear warheads against high-value targets.

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: Remarkable Accuracy

Prithvi became renowned for its precision. Equipped with a strap-down Inertial Navigation System and onboard trajectory correction computers, later variants achieved a Circular Error Probable (CEP) of approximately 10–50 meters; making it one of the most accurate ballistic missiles of its generation.

8/14

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: The Liquid Fuel Challenge

The missile used Inhibited Red Fuming Nitric Acid (IRFNA) as an oxidizer and a fuel mixture based on xylidine and triethylamine. While powerful, the propellants were highly corrosive and hazardous, requiring fueling shortly before launch and increasing preparation time in combat situations.

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: Under the Hood

Prithvi employed a single-stage liquid-fueled propulsion system influenced by technology derived from the Soviet SA-2 surface-to-air missile. Indian engineers extensively modified and adapted the design for surface-to-surface ballistic missile operations, creating an entirely new operational capability.

6/14

R to @NetramDefence: The Historic First Flight

On 25 February 1988, India successfully test-fired the Prithvi-I Short-Range Ballistic Missile from Sriharikota. The achievement marked India’s entry into the exclusive club of nations capable of designing, developing, and producing indigenous ballistic missiles.

R to @NetramDefence: Building Under Technology Denial

India’s missile ambitions developed in an era when advanced missile technologies were tightly restricted by international export-control regimes. Faced with limited access to foreign technology, Indian scientists were compelled to develop critical propulsion, guidance, and control systems largely through indigenous research and innovation.

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: Project Devil & The Blueprint

The origins of Prithvi can be traced to DRDO’s “Project Devil” of the 1970s and a proposal known as “A Missile Plan for 1980.” Initially designated SS-150, the project received top priority and became the first missile to move from concept to reality under IGMDP.

3/14

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: The IGMDP’s Five-Missile Vision

Prithvi was the first missile selected for development under IGMDP, alongside four other systems: Agni (ballistic missile), Akash (surface-to-air missile), Trishul (short-range SAM), and Nag (anti-tank missile). Together, they formed the foundation of India’s modern missile capabilities.
